title: Oak candle tray
subtitle: Seasoned oak tray with hand-cut chamfered edges
excerpt: A small oak tray with chamfered edges, sized to hold three or four pillar candles. Cut from a single seasoned oak board; the chamfers are hand-cut with a sloyd knife. A first project in chamfered edge work that introduces the controlled angled cut.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on small board work and chamfered edges. Newey and Drage on hand-cut chamfers without a moulding plane.

content.text: A candle tray sits on a sideboard or a low table and holds a small grouping of pillar candles: three or four 60 mm pillars, or a row of tea lights in metal cups. The form is a flat oak board with chamfered edges and a slight ridge along the long sides to keep wax from running off. Oak is the choice: dense, fire-resistant, and beautiful with age.
Seasoned
board because the tray sits flat indoors and the edges must remain straight.
The wood

A single seasoned oak board: 250 mm by 120 mm by 22 mm thick. Buy PAR oak from a timber yard or use an offcut from a previous project. The board must be flat; check by laying it on the bench and looking for daylight under the edges. A slight cup is acceptable; a twist is not. Beech or ash work as alternatives with similar density.
Tools
A hand saw if the board needs cutting to size, a
sloyd knife

for the chamfers, an optional flat spokeshave for the surface, a marking gauge, a try-square, a strop, and pure tung oil for the finish. Pure tung is chosen for heat. The set oil holds up to a warm candle base. Shellac and polyurethane do not.
Workspace
A stable bench, the board clamped flat or held in a vice. Good light. The chamfer cuts are short and controlled; the work-holding is what makes them clean.
Method
Default to the
push cut
throughout. Strop every 15 minutes.
Saw the board to size if it is oversized: 250 mm long, 120 mm wide. Check both ends are square with the try-square. Light sand all the saw cuts with 120 grit to remove the saw marks.
Mark the
chamfer

lines with the marking gauge. Set the gauge to 8 mm and score a line on the top face of the board, 8 mm in from each edge. Then set the gauge to the same 8 mm and score a line on each edge, 8 mm down from the top face. The two scored lines on each edge define the chamfer: a 45 degree cut from the top-face line to the edge-face line.

Cut the chamfer on one long edge with the sloyd knife. Hold the knife at 45 degrees to the top face, the cut path running parallel to the edge. Take light push cuts, starting at one end of the edge and working steadily to the other end. The cut peels a long thin shaving off the corner; aim for shavings that are even in width over the full length.

Refine the chamfer with further light push cuts until both scribed lines are reached. The chamfer surface should be flat and a consistent width along the full length of the edge. If the spokeshave is to hand, two passes will level any unevenness; if not, the knife alone is enough.

Repeat the chamfer on the second long edge, then the two short edges. Where the chamfers meet at the corners, the cut leaves a small triangular facet; this looks correct and is the traditional finish for a hand-cut chamfered tray.

Run two ridge cuts down the long sides of the tray. With the marking gauge set to 35 mm from each long edge, score a line on the top face. Pare a shallow 1 mm gulley along each scribed line with the sloyd knife held vertical. The two ridges run from end to end and frame a 50 mm wide central trough where the candles sit.
Finishing

Sand through 120, 180, then 240 grit. Apply pure tung oil in three thin coats, allowing 2 days between coats. The full cure for heat resistance is 3 weeks; until then, place a small ceramic disc under each candle to catch wax. After the cure, the oak is heat-tolerant up to the temperature of a small flame; any pillar candle is fine. A free-burning fire on the tray is not.
Variations
Long tray for a dining table: 400 mm by 120 mm, same construction. Holds 6 pillar candles in a row.
Walnut instead of oak: a darker, finer-grained tray. Walnut releases a black stain when freshly cut against iron tools; wipe down with citric-acid solution before oiling.

Inlaid line: pare a 2 mm groove between the central trough and the ridges and inlay a thin strip of a contrasting wood (sycamore in walnut, walnut in oak). The inlay is glued in with PVA and trimmed with the knife.
Care

Wipe with a damp cloth to remove dust. Scrape any wax drips with the back of a butter knife when cold; warm wax pulls off cold oak cleanly without lifting the finish. Re-oil once a year with a single coat of pure tung oil.

items.fix: Reset the angle for each cut by sighting the blade against the scribed lines. The spokeshave is useful here: it rides on the chamfer surface and self-levels.
items.cause: Knife angle shifted between cuts; not held at a consistent 45 degrees
items.symptom: Chamfer is uneven in width along its length

items.fix: Reverse the cut direction on the offending edge. Oak grain often switches direction across the board; chamfer cuts may need to be made in different directions on different edges.
items.cause: Cut running uphill on a grain slope
items.symptom: Chamfer cut tears against the grain

items.fix: Start and end the groove with light cuts; build depth in the middle only. The shape can be evened out with a few extra passes at the ends after the middle is cut.
items.cause: Knife wobble at the ends of the cut
items.symptom: Ridge groove is shallow at the ends and deep in the middle

items.fix: Re-coat with a single thin layer of pure tung oil. The cured surface accepts a recoat without sanding.
items.cause: Cure incomplete or finish worn by dusting
items.symptom: Surface dulls after a few months on the sideboard
